main_summary.summary: Leesco Guster (born March 26, 1936, Port Gibson, Mississippi) was interviewed by Emilye Crosby on December 3, 2015, at her home in Port Gibson, with John Bishop and Guha Shankar present. Guster grew up in Port Gibson and Vicksburg, did domestic work from age 16, lived in Chicago through the 1950s, then returned to Port Gibson in 1959 to marry contractor Harry Guster. While working as a domestic for Hillary, the daughter of her employer Mr. Walk-Cut, Guster began going each evening to McFatter's drugstore where Rudy Shields and federal voter-registration observers gathered. She drove Shields throughout Claiborne County in her 1965 Pontiac Catalina to canvass for voter registration, and she describes a near-confrontation with an armed white landowner at Rocky Springs. Guster hosted civil rights lawyers and Tucki Tate at her home; the household was threatened by phone by a self-identified KKK caller during a planned Charles Evers banquet, and they kept armed watch at night. She testified in NAACP v. Claiborne Hardware, the boycott case that reached the U.S. Supreme Court in 1982. She names Lily Mae Williams, Mrs. Wells, Reverend Dawson, A.C. Garner, Alberta Colmar, Kenneth Ross, Theo Black, and Thomas Russell as participants in Port Gibson NAACP organizing.
key_themes: Black women's domestic labor and political consciousness
main_summary.historical_significance: Guster is a rank-and-file Port Gibson NAACP organizer whose testimony grounds the canonical southwest Mississippi voter-registration and boycott narrative in granular local detail: the McFatter's drugstore staging point for federal voter registrars, the Rocky Springs canvassing confrontation, the Tuesday-night mass meetings at the church, and the household-level threats from a self-identified KKK caller during a planned Charles Evers banquet. Her firsthand account of testifying in NAACP v. Claiborne Hardware (the case that reached the U.S. Supreme Court in 1982) preserves a litigant-perspective record of the boycott-as-First-Amendment-activity argument from the Black community side. Guster also documents the internal tensions within the Port Gibson movement -- her disagreement with the second boycott extending to merchants who had complied, her objections to A.C. Garner's proposals to add dances to mass meetings, and her account of Reverend Dawson's reasons for stepping down as NAACP president. Her recall of the Mrs. Bear (mayor's wife) sponsorship of her teenage independence and her later domestic-work dismissal by Hillary after she persisted in voter-registration work captures the cross-racial domestic-labor relationships that structured small-town Mississippi life. Identifications of Theo Black, Thomas Russell, Kenneth Ross, A.C. Garner, and Alberta Colmar provide a roster of local Port Gibson activists not heavily represented in the canonical scholarship.

chapters.title: Early Life in Port Gibson and Vicksburg
chapters.summary: Guster was born March 26, 1936 in Port Gibson, Mississippi to a single mother who left for Vicksburg when Guster was six and later brought her there. She attended schools in Vicksburg through the fifth grade at Magnolia, then transferred for higher grades because of how the school grades were structured.

chapters.title: Fair Street and Chicago Years
chapters.summary: Guster moved to Port Gibson in 1953, describes the busy Fair Street (then called Negro Street) with cafes like Peaches and the social life of young Black Port Gibson. She moved to Chicago, worked at Montgomery Ward briefly until illness, then did day work, until returning to Port Gibson at age 19.

chapters.title: Marriage to Harry Guster
chapters.summary: Returning from Chicago on a three-week vacation in 1959, Guster married contractor Harry Guster, who was 24 years older and the head lumber contractor in Claiborne County. She negotiated his divorce and the support arrangements for his son, and they bought the house at $2,500 with money from his sister Mrs. Akon.

chapters.title: Working for Hillary and Movement Awakening
chapters.summary: Guster worked as a domestic for Hillary, the daughter of her employer Mr. Walk-Cut, who had moved from Mansfield, Ohio. While working there, Guster began driving each evening to McFatter's drugstore where Rudy Shields and federal voter-registration observers gathered, and she canvassed throughout Claiborne County. Hillary eventually fired her saying she was tired of the voter-registration activity.

chapters.title: Rocky Springs Voter-Registration Confrontation
chapters.summary: Guster drove Rudy Shields in her 1965 Pontiac Catalina through Claiborne County canvassing for voter registration. At Rocky Springs the white landowner met them with a shotgun on his pickup truck; after the encounter the landowner trailed them and parked beside a one-way bridge before letting them pass. Plantation residents were often too afraid to register, and Guster describes wearing out a set of tires driving the canvassing routes.

chapters.title: Hosting Movement Workers and Mass Meetings
chapters.summary: Guster and her husband hosted civil rights lawyers and the African civil rights worker Tucki Tate, sometimes three at a time. Lily Mae Williams was NAACP chairperson and Mrs. Wells served as cold chair. Guster led the youth singing freedom songs including 'Ain't Gonna Let Nobody Turn Me Around,' 'Oh Freedom,' and 'He's Got the Whole World in His Hands,' meeting Tuesday nights at the church.
related_events: civil rights legal teams in Mississippi

chapters.title: KKK Threats and Armed Defense
chapters.summary: Before a planned Charles Evers banquet at the Guster home, a caller identifying himself as KKK asked Guster if she was tired of living and threatened to burn the house and the Memphis house where Rudy Shields was staying. The household kept armed watch that night; Rudy Shields slept in the back of the house with shotguns. Guster describes nearly firing her own pistol through the mattress in fear, and her husband emerging convinced she had killed herself.
related_events: Klan intimidation of Port Gibson NAACP

chapters.title: Boycott, Claiborne Hardware Case, and Deacons
chapters.summary: Guster describes the Port Gibson boycott of white merchants, the Deacons for Defense organizing meetings under an oak tree on her property, and her disagreement with the second boycott extending to merchants who had complied. She testified in NAACP v. Claiborne Hardware against Allen Motors employee Buster Barber, recalling Mr. Eddie Walls' and Mr. Dan Curry's testimony when the case reached the U.S. Supreme Court in 1982.

chapters.title: Political Organizing and Black Candidates
chapters.summary: Guster discusses the first Black candidates elected to Claiborne County office, her preference for Thomas Russell over Theo Black, and her conviction that Black political success required cross-racial coalitions. She names Mr. Williams as a backbone of Black political organizing in Port Gibson and reflects that Russell would not accept conditional white-establishment endorsements.
related_events: Black electoral politics in post-1965 Mississippi

chapters.title: Mural, Mr. Nate, and Reflection
chapters.summary: Kenneth Ross asked Guster to provide names for the Port Gibson Civil Rights mural; she gave him ten male names but said the women she knew mainly talked at meetings. Her own face was added to the mural without her knowledge, possibly by Mr. Nate. She credits Nate Jones with preserving her movement history and reflects on what young people should learn: speak up and work, do not just talk.
related_events: Port Gibson civil rights mural project

loc_video.caption: Leesco Guster oral history interview conducted by Emilye Crosby in Port Gibson, Mississippi, 2015 December 03
